text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

Emily Carter, DOB: 22-07-1992, Hospital No: U914527 NHS No. 1045928376  
Flat 3, 28 Northway Court, London, NW1 6QP 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with suspected catamenial pattern/primary generalised tonic-clonic seizures and brief focal features reported by witness/iron deficiency anaemia under GP review/migraine without aura. 

Medication: Sodium valproate modified-release 500 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte (intermittent perimenstrual use as rescue per prior plan).  
Ferrous fumarate 210 mg once daily.  
Sumatriptan 50 mg as required for migraine.  

I reviewed her in clinic today unaccompanied. She remains independent with daily activities and continues part-time work in retail. She has been using a menstrual tracking application consistently for the past 9 months and allowed me to review the app entries on her phone during the consultation. Energy levels fluctuate around menstruation; otherwise mood and sleep are stable. No recent intercurrent illness. 

Seizures: She describes short spells characterised by a sudden pause with fixed gaze and small chewing-like mouth movements, followed by quick recovery without confusion. These “brief pause-and-stare episodes with subtle mouth movements” occur predominantly in clusters within the 3 days prior to menses and the first 2 days of flow. The app logs correlate: over the last 6 cycles she recorded 3–6 of these brief episodes per cycle within that 5-day perimenstrual window, and none or at most one isolated episode outside it. In addition, there have been two generalised tonic-clonic seizures this year (February and July), each on day −1 to day +1 relative to menses onset, both occurring in the early morning. No injuries, tongue bite once, transient post-ictal myalgia, no incontinence. No known triggers aside from the cyclical pattern; she avoids alcohol and has been adherent to medication. Witness accounts are consistent with generalised onset; there is no history of focal neurological deficits preceding events. 

Given the history, examination, past EEG reports (previous routine EEG with generalised spike–wave discharges) and seizure semiology, this fits with generalised epilepsy, with catamenial exacerbation supported by contemporaneous app records. 

Plan:  
- Continue sodium valproate MR 500 mg twice daily; adherence reinforced.  
- Perimenstrual clobazam as previously advised: 10 mg nightly from day −3 to day +2 relative to menses; she has found this reduces clustering without daytime sedation.  
- Continue menstrual tracking app and bring screenshots to next review; patient will tag any seizure-day with “C1–C5” to denote cycle day for clearer correlation.  
- Safety advice reiterated including bathing and height precautions; no driving as per DVLA regulations until seizure-free by required period; she understands.  
- Bloods via GP: FBC, LFTs, and valproate level (trough) in the next 2 weeks; continue iron therapy and recheck ferritin in 3 months.  
- Consider addition of lamotrigine as adjunct if catamenial pattern persists despite current perimenstrual clobazam; to be discussed at next visit.  
- Review in neurology clinic in 4 months, sooner if breakthrough generalised tonic-clonic seizures occur.  

Yours sincerely

entities.text: over the last 6 cycles she recorded 3–6 of these brief episodes per cycle within that 5-day perimenstrual window, and none or at most one isolated episode outside it.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter reports two seizure types. The brief pause-and-stare episodes occur mainly perimenstrually with explicit counts: "3–6 ... per cycle within that 5-day perimenstrual window" and additionally "none or at most one" outside that window per cycle. Therefore total per cycle = 3 to 6 + 0 to 1 = 3 to 7 per cycle. A menstrual cycle is approximately monthly, so this maps to 3 to 7 per month. There were also two generalised tonic-clonic seizures this year (2 per year), but per instruction we choose the highest frequency, which is the brief episodes at 3 to 7 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: over the last 6 cycles she recorded 3–6 of these brief episodes per cycle within that 5-day perimenstrual window, and none or at most one isolated episode outside it.
reference: Behavioral arrest seizure episodes with oral automatisms

flaws.description: Gold (3 to 7 per month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
